text.title: Multimodal VUI Design Core Rules
text.summary: Six testable rules grounding the multimodal voice-UI spec. Every rule pairs a binary check with a rationale and an attributable source.

rule.statement: The spec MUST declare a Word Error Rate (WER) budget for each target language and a recovery ladder for utterances that exceed it. WER without a recovery ladder is a half-spec.

rationale.source: ASR research; Apple Siri / Google Assistant published guidelines

rule.rationale: Real-world WER varies by language, accent, and noise. Without a budget the team ships voice features that fail silently on minority accents; without a recovery ladder the same utterance loops forever instead of falling back to visual or a different intent.

rule.statement: Persona voice constraints (pitch range, formality, locale-specific cadence) MUST be signed off by brand before TTS samples ship to engineering. Default vendor voices are not a persona decision.

rationale.source: Brand voice audits 2024-2025; Mailchimp voice & tone guidelines

rule.rationale: Default TTS voices erode brand equity — every product sounds the same. Forcing brand sign-off at spec time prevents post-launch persona disputes that block releases.

rule.statement: The spec MUST list languages covered AND languages explicitly out-of-scope. Implicit "we'll add more later" without a coverage policy is FORBIDDEN.

rationale.source: i18n product audits; EU Digital Services Act language guidance

rule.rationale: Voice products that ship "English first, others later" without a documented coverage policy accumulate broken edge cases for non-English users that look like quality regressions instead of scope decisions.

rule.statement: The spec MUST define a fallback ladder: ASR retry → confirm → visual readout → human-or-other-modality. Each rung names its trigger and the dwell time before falling further.

rationale.source: Multimodal interaction research; in-car VUI usability studies

rule.rationale: Without an explicit ladder, error states become "model retries until the user gives up". A documented ladder is the difference between a polite VUI and a frustrating one.

rule.statement: Visual or haptic affordances that support voice MUST be specified per task — including reduced-visual mode, haptic-only confirmations, and screen-reader parity for the same intent.

rationale.source: WCAG 2.2 Success Criteria 2.5.4 (Motion Actuation), 1.3.5 (Identify Input Purpose); inclusive design research

rule.rationale: Voice-only does not satisfy accessibility for deaf-hard-of-hearing users; visual-only excludes blind users. Multimodal means parity across modalities, not voice with optional visual sugar.

rule.statement: When the experience runs inside a third-party voice platform (Alexa, Google Assistant), the spec MUST document which rules the platform owns and which the team owns. Don't re-document what the platform enforces; do document what the platform leaves to the team.

rationale.source: Alexa Skills Kit + Google Assistant SDK docs; cross-platform VUI audits

rule.rationale: Specs that duplicate platform-owned rules go stale every time the platform changes — and the team owns the maintenance burden. Specs that omit team-owned rules ship platform-default behaviour as if intentional.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the Applies-If preconditions are not met OR any Skip-If disqualifier holds.

rationale.source: F-066 v3 B5.6 — mandatory skip leaf

rule.rationale: Decision-tree leaves must reference a rule id. The skip leaf needs a real rule so non-applicable inputs route cleanly out instead of producing false-positive verdicts.
